items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.q: In sonata form, in what key does the second subject normally appear in the exposition of a piece in a major key?
items.why: It's Dominant. “Tonic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer is credited with significantly expanding the coda in sonata-form movements?
items.why: It's Beethoven. “Mozart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main sections does classical sonata form contain (exposition, development, recapitulation)?
items.why: The real figure is 3 sections. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
